title: Transcatheter or Surgical Aortic-Valve Replacement in Low-Risk Patients at 7 Years
key_finding: Among low-risk patients with severe, symptomatic aortic stenosis, no significant differences in composite endpoints of death, stroke, and rehospitalization were observed at 7 years between TAVR and surgical aortic-valve replacement. Bioprosthetic valve failure rates were similar (6.9% TAVR vs 7.5% surgery).
clinical_implications: TAVR continues to be a viable long-term alternative to surgery in low-risk patients with severe aortic stenosis with similar durability and clinical outcomes at 7 years.

title: Transcatheter Valve Replacement in Severe Tricuspid Regurgitation
key_finding: Transcatheter tricuspid-valve replacement was superior to medical therapy alone with a win ratio of 2.02, driven primarily by improvements in symptoms and quality of life. The valve-replacement group had higher rates of severe bleeding (15.4% vs 5.3%) and new permanent pacemakers (17.4% vs 2.3%).
clinical_implications: This establishes transcatheter tricuspid-valve replacement as an effective treatment option for patients with severe tricuspid regurgitation, though with increased procedural risks.

title: Transcatheter Aortic-Valve Replacement for Asymptomatic Severe Aortic Stenosis
key_finding: Among patients with asymptomatic severe aortic stenosis, early TAVR was superior to clinical surveillance in reducing the composite endpoint of death, stroke, or unplanned hospitalization for cardiovascular causes (26.8% vs 45.3%, HR 0.50, P<0.001).
clinical_implications: This study supports early intervention with TAVR in asymptomatic severe aortic stenosis patients rather than the current guideline-recommended approach of routine clinical surveillance.

title: Outcomes of Balloon-Expandable Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement in Younger Patients in the Low-Risk Era
key_finding: Among patients receiving balloon-expandable TAVR, those younger than 65 years (5.7% of cohort) had higher comorbidity burden and significantly higher 1-year mortality (9.9% vs 8.2%) and readmission rates (28.2% vs 26.1%) compared to patients aged 65-80 years.
clinical_implications: These findings support heart team decision-making for TAVR in younger patients with high comorbidity burden and suggest that current clinical practice appropriately reserves TAVR for younger patients with limited life expectancy.

title: Three-Year Follow-Up of the NOTION-2 Trial: TAVR Versus SAVR to Treat Younger Low-Risk Patients With Tricuspid or Bicuspid Aortic Stenosis
key_finding: At 3 years, clinical outcomes were similar between TAVR and surgery in patients age 60-75 years with severe aortic stenosis at low surgical risk, with both procedures showing low rates of structural valve deterioration and reintervention.
clinical_implications: TAVR appears to be a viable alternative to surgery for younger, low-risk patients with severe aortic stenosis, though further long-term follow-up is needed to guide treatment decisions in this population.

title: Myocardial Fibrosis and Timing of Intervention for Aortic Stenosis
key_finding: The EVOLVED trial found no significant difference in the primary composite endpoint between early AVR and conservative management in asymptomatic severe aortic stenosis patients with myocardial fibrosis. Secondary endpoints of unplanned hospitalizations and symptom burden favored early AVR but were not definitive.
clinical_implications: Current evidence does not support routine screening with MRI for myocardial fibrosis to guide early AVR in asymptomatic patients with severe aortic stenosis.

title: Early Intervention in Patients With Asymptomatic Severe Aortic Stenosis and Myocardial Fibrosis: The EVOLVED Randomized Clinical Trial
key_finding: Early aortic valve intervention had no demonstrable effect on all-cause death or unplanned aortic stenosis-related hospitalization in asymptomatic patients with severe aortic stenosis and myocardial fibrosis. Early intervention was associated with lower rates of NYHA class II-IV symptoms at 12 months.
clinical_implications: These results suggest that in asymptomatic severe aortic stenosis patients with myocardial fibrosis, early valve intervention may not improve hard clinical endpoints, though the trial had wide confidence intervals requiring further research.

title: Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement in Patients With Systolic Heart Failure and Moderate Aortic Stenosis
key_finding: TAVR was not superior to aortic stenosis surveillance for the primary hierarchical composite endpoint in patients with moderate AS and heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. However, preemptive TAVR provided clinically meaningful quality-of-life benefits at 1 year.
clinical_implications: This study suggests that routine preemptive TAVR for moderate aortic stenosis in heart failure patients may not improve major clinical outcomes but could be considered for quality-of-life benefits in selected patients.

title: TAVR in Patients with Moderate Aortic Stenosis and Heart Failure
key_finding: The TAVR UNLOAD trial, the first randomized trial evaluating TAVR in moderate AS with reduced ejection fraction, failed to demonstrate superiority of early TAVR over guideline-directed medical therapy alone for the primary composite endpoint. However, TAVR showed greater improvement in quality of life scores and no increase in mortality or stroke.
clinical_implications: This editorial highlights the need for further research into early TAVR intervention for moderate aortic stenosis with heart failure, as current evidence remains inconclusive despite promising quality of life improvements.

title: Transcatheter vs Surgical Aortic Valve Replacement in Bicuspid Aortic Valves
key_finding: Among Medicare beneficiaries with bicuspid aortic valves, TAVR was associated with lower procedural mortality but higher rates of pacemaker implantation, longitudinal stroke, and all-cause mortality compared with SAVR. Even in young low-risk patients, TAVR showed higher longitudinal stroke rates and composite outcomes.
clinical_implications: These findings suggest SAVR may provide superior long-term outcomes in bicuspid aortic valve patients, particularly young low-risk patients, despite higher procedural mortality.

title: Bicuspid aortic stenosis: National three-year outcomes of transcatheter versus surgical aortic valve replacement among Medicare beneficiaries
key_finding: Among matched Medicare beneficiaries with bicuspid aortic stenosis, TAVR was associated with higher 3-year mortality compared to SAVR, but similar mortality risk and lower heart failure readmissions in the first 6 months.
clinical_implications: TAVR may be safe in selected patients with bicuspid AS and favorable anatomy, but randomized comparative data are needed to best inform treatment choice.

title: Surgical vs transcatheter aortic valve replacement in bicuspid aortic valve stenosis: A systematic review and meta-analysis
key_finding: SAVR and TAVR had similar in-hospital mortality and stroke rates in bicuspid aortic stenosis, but SAVR had lower rates of permanent pacemaker implantation and paravalvular leak while TAVR had lower rates of acute kidney injury, major bleeding, and pulmonary complications.
clinical_implications: Despite similar early mortality, the increased risk of permanent pacemaker implantation and paravalvular leak with TAVR suggests caution should be exercised in widespread adoption of TAVR in lower surgical-risk bicuspid aortic stenosis patients.

title: Transcatheter aortic valve implantation in low-risk tricuspid or bicuspid aortic stenosis: the NOTION-2 trial
key_finding: Among low-risk patients aged ≤75 years with severe symptomatic aortic stenosis, the 1-year composite rate of death, stroke, or rehospitalization was similar between TAVI (10.2%) and surgery (7.1%) groups (HR 1.4, 95% CI 0.7-2.9, P=0.3). TAVI outcomes in bicuspid AS patients showed numerically higher event rates and warrant caution.
clinical_implications: TAVI appears to be a reasonable alternative to surgery in young, low-risk patients with tricuspid aortic stenosis, but caution should be exercised when considering TAVI for bicuspid aortic stenosis.

title: Five-Year Follow-up after Transcatheter Repair of Secondary Mitral Regurgitation
key_finding: Transcatheter edge-to-edge repair of the mitral valve led to a lower rate of hospitalization for heart failure (33.1% vs 57.2% per year) and lower all-cause mortality (57.3% vs 67.2%) through 5 years compared to medical therapy alone in patients with heart failure and secondary mitral regurgitation.
clinical_implications: This establishes the long-term durability of transcatheter mitral valve repair benefits in heart failure patients with secondary mitral regurgitation, supporting its use as a standard treatment option.

title: Transcatheter Repair for Patients with Tricuspid Regurgitation
key_finding: Tricuspid TEER was safe and associated with improved quality of life compared to medical therapy, with 87.0% of TEER patients achieving tricuspid regurgitation of no greater than moderate severity at 30 days versus 4.8% in the control group.
clinical_implications: Tricuspid TEER provides a safe alternative to medical therapy alone for patients with severe tricuspid regurgitation, offering meaningful improvement in quality of life and reduction in regurgitation severity.

title: Transcatheter Aortic-Valve Replacement in Low-Risk Patients at Five Years
key_finding: Among low-risk patients with severe, symptomatic aortic stenosis who underwent TAVR or surgery, there was no significant between-group difference in the two primary composite outcomes at 5 years. The composite end point occurred in 22.8% in the TAVR group versus 27.2% in the surgery group.
clinical_implications: TAVR and surgical aortic valve replacement demonstrate similar long-term outcomes at 5 years in low-risk patients, supporting TAVR as an equivalent treatment option for this population.

title: Transcatheter Aortic-Valve Replacement in Low-Risk Patients at Five Years
key_finding: Among low-risk patients with severe, symptomatic aortic stenosis who underwent TAVR or surgery, there was no significant between-group difference in the two primary composite outcomes at 5 years. The composite end point occurred in 22.8% of TAVR patients vs 27.2% of surgery patients (P=0.07).
clinical_implications: TAVR and surgical aortic valve replacement show similar long-term outcomes in low-risk patients, supporting TAVR as an equivalent treatment option for this population.

title: Transcatheter Aortic-Valve Replacement in Low-Risk Patients at Five Years
key_finding: Among low-risk patients with severe, symptomatic aortic stenosis, there was no significant between-group difference in the two primary composite outcomes between TAVR and surgical aortic-valve replacement at 5 years. Bioprosthetic-valve failure occurred in 3.3% of TAVR patients and 3.8% of surgery patients.
clinical_implications: TAVR remains a viable alternative to surgical aortic valve replacement in low-risk patients with comparable long-term outcomes through 5 years.

title: Indication Creep in Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation—Data or Desire?
key_finding: Despite FDA approval of TAVI for all risk levels based on low-risk trials, significant knowledge gaps exist regarding use in patients <65 years, those with bicuspid valves, substantial CAD, and concomitant valve disease. The trials excluded many real-world patients and had highly selective populations.
clinical_implications: Clinicians should carefully consider existing knowledge gaps and patient-specific factors when selecting TAVI vs SAVR, particularly in younger patients where long-term durability data are limited.

title: Structural Valve Deterioration After Self-Expanding Transcatheter or Surgical Aortic Valve Implantation in Patients at Intermediate or High Risk
key_finding: The cumulative incidence of structural valve deterioration at 5 years was significantly lower in patients undergoing self-expanding TAVI compared to surgery (2.20% vs 4.38%, HR 0.46, P=0.004). SVD was associated with increased 5-year all-cause mortality and heart failure hospitalizations.
clinical_implications: Self-expanding TAVI demonstrates superior valve durability compared to surgery at 5 years, particularly in patients with smaller annuli, supporting its use in intermediate and high-risk patients.

title: Concomitant Tricuspid Repair in Patients with Degenerative Mitral Regurgitation
key_finding: Patients who underwent mitral-valve surgery plus tricuspid annuloplasty had fewer primary-end-point events than those who underwent mitral-valve surgery alone (3.9% vs. 10.2%), driven by less frequent progression to severe tricuspid regurgitation.
clinical_implications: Concomitant tricuspid repair during mitral valve surgery reduces tricuspid regurgitation progression but increases permanent pacemaker implantation risk.

title: Cerebral Embolic Protection during Transcatheter Aortic-Valve Replacement
key_finding: Among patients undergoing transfemoral TAVR, cerebral embolic protection did not significantly reduce the incidence of periprocedural stroke within 72 hours (2.3% vs 2.9%, P=0.30). However, disabling stroke was numerically lower in the CEP group (0.5% vs 1.3%).
clinical_implications: The routine use of cerebral embolic protection during TAVR cannot be definitively recommended based on these results, though a potential benefit cannot be ruled out.

title: The PARTNER 3 Bicuspid Registry for Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement in Low-Surgical-Risk Patients
key_finding: In highly selected bicuspid aortic stenosis patients at low surgical risk, TAVR with the SAPIEN 3 valve demonstrated similar 1-year outcomes (death, stroke, cardiovascular rehospitalization) compared to matched tricuspid aortic valve patients (10.9% vs 10.2%, p=0.80).
clinical_implications: TAVR with the SAPIEN 3 valve can be considered in carefully selected bicuspid aortic stenosis patients at low surgical risk without extensive raphe or subannular calcification.

title: Propensity-Matched 1-Year Outcomes Following Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement in Low-Risk Bicuspid and Tricuspid Patients
key_finding: There were no significant differences in clinical or forward flow hemodynamic outcomes between propensity-matched bicuspid and tricuspid aortic stenosis patients at 1 year after TAVR. Bicuspid patients had fewer paravalvular leaks (21.3% vs 42.6%, P<0.001) with similar mortality rates.
clinical_implications: TAVR in low-risk bicuspid aortic stenosis patients appears to be as safe and effective as in tricuspid patients, supporting expansion of TAVR to this previously excluded population.

title: Self-expanding Transcatheter vs Surgical Aortic Valve Replacement in Intermediate-Risk Patients: 5-Year Outcomes of the SURTAVI Randomized Clinical Trial
key_finding: Among intermediate-risk patients with symptomatic severe aortic stenosis, major clinical outcomes at 5 years were similar for TAVR and surgery, with similar rates of death or disabling stroke (TAVR 31.3% vs surgery 30.8%). TAVR was associated with superior hemodynamic valve performance but also with more paravalvular leak and valve reinterventions.
clinical_implications: Both TAVR and surgery remain viable options for intermediate-risk patients with severe aortic stenosis, with treatment selection requiring consideration of hemodynamic benefits versus increased reintervention rates with TAVR.

title: Long-term Outcomes of Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement With the Lotus Valve vs CoreValve/EvolutR: A Secondary Analysis of the REPRISE III Randomized Clinical Trial
key_finding: At 5 years, the Lotus valve had similar mortality rates (50.9% vs 52.8%) and comparable overall outcomes to CoreValve/EvolutR, with fewer disabling strokes but higher rates of pacemaker implantation and prosthetic valve thrombosis.
clinical_implications: The Lotus valve demonstrates comparable long-term safety and effectiveness to CoreValve/EvolutR for transcatheter aortic valve replacement at 5-year follow-up.

title: Association Between Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement for Bicuspid vs Tricuspid Aortic Stenosis and Mortality or Stroke Among Patients at Low Surgical Risk
key_finding: In propensity-matched patients at low surgical risk, TAVR for bicuspid vs tricuspid aortic stenosis showed no significant differences in mortality or stroke at 30 days or 1 year. There were also no significant differences in procedural complications, valve hemodynamics, or paravalvular leak rates.
clinical_implications: TAVR appears to have similar safety and efficacy outcomes for bicuspid and tricuspid aortic stenosis in low-risk patients, though randomized trials are needed for definitive assessment.

title: Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement in Low-Risk Bicuspid and Tricuspid Patients: Meta-Analysis
key_finding: In low-risk patients, TAVR outcomes at 30 days were comparable between bicuspid and tricuspid aortic stenosis patients, with similar mortality, stroke, and pacemaker implantation rates. There was a trend toward higher stroke in bicuspid patients.
clinical_implications: TAVR appears safe in low-risk bicuspid aortic stenosis patients with outcomes similar to tricuspid patients, though randomized trials are needed for long-term safety confirmation.

title: Outcomes After Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement in Bicuspid Versus Tricuspid Anatomy: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
key_finding: TAVR in bicuspid aortic valve anatomy had similar device success and 1-year survival rates compared to tricuspid anatomy, but was associated with higher rates of moderate to severe paravalvular leak, annular rupture, and cerebral ischemic events.
clinical_implications: TAVR is feasible in selected bicuspid aortic valve patients but requires caution due to increased procedural risks and warrants further evidence before widespread adoption.

original_filename: Journal of Cardiac Surgery - 2021 - Tsai - Major adverse cardiac events and functional capacity in patients at intermediate.pdf
title: Major adverse cardiac events and functional capacity in patients at intermediate risk undergoing transcatheter versus surgical aortic valve replacement for aortic stenosis with bicuspid valves
key_finding: SAVR was associated with better functional recovery (87.8% vs lower rates in TAVR group) while MACE rates were similar between groups in patients with bicuspid aortic valve stenosis. TAVR is an acceptable option for these patients though SAVR provides superior functional outcomes.
clinical_implications: TAVR is a viable alternative to SAVR in bicuspid aortic valve patients, but early interventions to improve functional capacity should be considered for TAVR patients given their lower functional recovery rates.

title: Five-Year Health Status After Self-expanding Transcatheter or Surgical Aortic Valve Replacement in High-risk Patients With Severe Aortic Stenosis
key_finding: In high-risk patients with severe aortic stenosis, there was an early health status benefit with self-expanding iliofemoral TAVR vs SAVR but no difference between groups in long-term health status over 5 years. Although mortality at 5 years was high in this population, the majority of surviving patients continued to report reasonable health status.
clinical_implications: The early health status advantage of TAVR over SAVR does not persist long-term in high-risk patients, suggesting that valve choice should consider patient-specific factors beyond health status outcomes.

title: Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement in Low-risk Patients With Bicuspid Aortic Valve Stenosis
key_finding: TAVR in low-risk bicuspid aortic stenosis patients achieved a 1.3% rate of all-cause mortality or disabling stroke at 30 days with a 95.3% device success rate.
clinical_implications: TAVR appears to be a safe and effective treatment option for low-risk patients with bicuspid aortic valve stenosis.

title: Five-Year Outcomes of Transcatheter or Surgical Aortic-Valve Replacement
key_finding: At 5 years, there was no significant difference in death or disabling stroke between TAVR and surgical aortic valve replacement in intermediate-risk patients (47.9% vs 43.4%, HR 1.09, 95% CI 0.95-1.25, P=0.21). However, TAVR was associated with more paravalvular regurgitation, repeat hospitalizations, and valve reinterventions.
clinical_implications: TAVR provides similar long-term survival outcomes to surgery in intermediate-risk aortic stenosis patients, but with higher rates of paravalvular leak and reinterventions that require careful patient selection and follow-up.

title: Transcatheter aortic valve replacement in patients with bicuspid aortic valve stenosis: national trends and in-hospital outcomes
key_finding: TAVR use for bicuspid aortic valve stenosis significantly increased from 0.39% in 2011 to 4.16% in 2014, with no statistically significant difference in in-hospital mortality between TAVR and SAVR groups (0% vs. 5.9%; adjusted P = 0.119).
clinical_implications: There is growing off-label use of TAVR for bicuspid aortic valve stenosis with comparable safety outcomes to surgical replacement, though surgical replacement remains the standard of care.

title: Outcomes of Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement in Patients With Bicuspid Aortic Valve Disease: A Report From the Society of Thoracic Surgeons/American College of Cardiology Transcatheter Valve Therapy Registry
key_finding: With current-generation TAVR devices, procedural and 1-year outcomes were comparable between bicuspid and tricuspid aortic valve stenosis, with device success rates of 96.3% versus 97.4% respectively. Current-generation devices showed improved outcomes in bicuspid valves compared to older-generation devices, with reduced moderate/severe aortic insufficiency (2.7% vs 14.0%).
clinical_implications: TAVR is now a viable treatment option for patients with bicuspid aortic valve disease when using newer-generation devices.

title: Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement for Patients at Low Surgical Risk—Selective or Ubiquitous?
key_finding: TAVR demonstrated superiority or non-inferiority to surgery in low-risk patients across two major randomized trials, with reduced stroke, rehospitalization, and procedural complications. However, durability data beyond 1-2 years in younger, low-risk patients remains unavailable.
clinical_implications: TAVR-first strategy is emerging for low-risk aortic stenosis patients, but careful patient selection and shared decision-making remain critical, especially for younger patients where durability concerns persist.

title: Transcatheter Aortic-Valve Replacement in Low-Risk Patients
key_finding: Two recent trials confirmed superiority or noninferiority of TAVR compared to surgical aortic valve replacement in low-risk patients at 1-2 year follow-up, though with smaller sample sizes and fewer primary endpoint events than previous intermediate-risk cohorts.
clinical_implications: The extension of TAVR indication to low-risk patients requires more stringent trial design with unbiased endpoints and longer follow-up given that 80% of surgical aortic valve procedures are performed in low-risk patients.

title: Transcatheter Aortic-Valve Replacement with a Balloon-Expandable Valve in Low-Risk Patients
key_finding: Among low-risk patients with severe aortic stenosis, TAVR resulted in significantly lower rates of the composite endpoint of death, stroke, or rehospitalization at 1 year compared to surgery (8.5% vs. 15.1%). TAVR also showed superiority in 30-day outcomes including lower stroke rates and shorter hospitalizations.
clinical_implications: This trial establishes TAVR as a superior treatment option to surgery for low-risk patients with severe aortic stenosis, expanding TAVR indications to the broadest patient population.

title: Procedural Volume and Outcomes for Transcatheter Aortic-Valve Replacement
key_finding: An inverse volume-mortality association was observed for transfemoral TAVR procedures, with 30-day mortality higher at low-volume hospitals (3.19%) compared to high-volume hospitals (2.66%), representing a 19.45% relative mortality reduction.
clinical_implications: These findings support volume requirements for TAVR programs as higher procedural volume is associated with improved patient outcomes and reduced mortality variability.

title: Bicuspid Aortic Valve Stenosis: Is There a Role for TAVR?
key_finding: Current-generation TAVR valves show similar 30-day and 1-year mortality in bicuspid vs trileaflet aortic valves, though stroke risk is higher at 30 days. These data suggest TAVR may be reasonable for selected bicuspid aortic valve patients.
clinical_implications: TAVR may be a viable option for patients with bicuspid aortic valves using current-generation devices, but careful anatomic selection and further study of different bicuspid phenotypes is needed.

title: Association Between Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement for Bicuspid vs Tricuspid Aortic Stenosis and Mortality or Stroke
key_finding: Patients with bicuspid vs tricuspid aortic stenosis had similar 30-day and 1-year mortality after TAVR but had increased 30-day stroke risk (2.5% vs 1.6%, HR 1.57). No significant differences were found in paravalvular leak or valve hemodynamics.
clinical_implications: TAVR appears feasible for bicuspid aortic stenosis but carries higher early stroke risk compared to tricuspid anatomy, requiring careful patient selection and randomized trials for definitive safety assessment.

title: Comparison of Outcomes After Transcatheter vs Surgical Aortic Valve Replacement Among Patients at Intermediate Operative Risk With a History of Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery
key_finding: Both TAVR and SAVR were safe for intermediate-risk patients with AS and prior CABG surgery, with no significant difference in all-cause mortality or disabling stroke at 1-year follow-up (8.9% vs 6.7%, P = .53). TAVR facilitated faster improvement in quality of life and better exercise capacity at 1-year follow-up.
clinical_implications: TAVR represents a viable alternative to SAVR for intermediate-risk aortic stenosis patients with prior CABG surgery, offering faster recovery and improved quality of life.

title: Two-Year Outcomes After Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement With Mechanical vs Self-expanding Valves: The REPRISE III Randomized Clinical Trial
key_finding: At 2 years, all-cause mortality rates were similar between Lotus and CoreValve (21.3% vs 22.5%), but disabling stroke was less frequent with Lotus (4.7% vs 8.6%) while CoreValve showed superior valve hemodynamics with larger valve areas and lower gradients.
clinical_implications: Both TAVR platforms show similar mortality outcomes at 2 years, with each having distinct advantages that should inform individualized valve selection based on patient-specific factors.

title: Transcatheter Mitral-Valve Repair in Patients with Heart Failure
key_finding: Transcatheter mitral-valve repair with MitraClip reduced heart failure hospitalizations (35.8% vs 67.9% per patient-year) and all-cause mortality (29.1% vs 46.1%) compared to medical therapy alone in patients with secondary mitral regurgitation.
clinical_implications: Transcatheter mitral valve repair should be considered in symptomatic heart failure patients with moderate-to-severe secondary mitral regurgitation despite optimal medical therapy.

title: Surgical or Transcatheter Aortic-Valve Replacement in Intermediate-Risk Patients
key_finding: TAVR was noninferior to surgery in patients with severe aortic stenosis at intermediate surgical risk, with the primary endpoint occurring in 12.6% of TAVR patients versus 14.0% of surgery patients at 24 months. Each procedure had a different pattern of adverse events, with surgery having higher rates of acute kidney injury, atrial fibrillation, and transfusion requirements, while TAVR had higher rates of residual aortic regurgitation and need for pacemaker implantation.
clinical_implications: TAVR represents a viable alternative to surgical valve replacement for intermediate-risk patients with severe aortic stenosis, expanding treatment options beyond high-risk populations.

title: Association Between Hospital Volume and 30-Day Readmissions Following Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement
key_finding: High-volume TAVR hospitals had significantly lower 30-day readmission rates compared to medium-volume (OR 0.76) and low-volume (OR 0.75) hospitals. This inverse association between hospital TAVR volume and readmissions resulted in lower healthcare system costs.
clinical_implications: TAVR patients may benefit from treatment at higher-volume centers to reduce readmission risk and healthcare costs.

title: Transcatheter or Surgical Aortic-Valve Replacement in Intermediate-Risk Patients
key_finding: In intermediate-risk patients with severe aortic stenosis, TAVR was noninferior to surgical aortic-valve replacement with respect to death or disabling stroke at 2 years (19.3% vs 21.1%). In the transfemoral-access cohort, TAVR showed superior outcomes compared to surgery.
clinical_implications: TAVR can be considered as an alternative to surgical aortic valve replacement in intermediate-risk patients, particularly those suitable for transfemoral access.

title: Outcomes in the Randomized CoreValve US Pivotal High Risk Trial in Patients With a Society of Thoracic Surgeons Risk Score of 7% or Less
key_finding: Self-expanding TAVR showed superior 2-year survival compared to SAVR (15.0% vs 26.3% mortality) in high-risk patients with STS PROM scores ≤7%, traditionally considered intermediate risk. Quality of life improvements were equivalent between groups.
clinical_implications: TAVR may be favorable over surgical AVR even in patients with lower surgical risk scores traditionally considered intermediate risk.

title: Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement in Bicuspid Aortic Valve Disease
key_finding: TAV-in-BAV is feasible with 3.6% procedural mortality and 17.5% one-year mortality, but 28.4% of patients had post-implantation AR ≥2+, which was reduced to 17.4% when MSCT-based sizing was used.
clinical_implications: MSCT-based TAV sizing should be strongly considered in bicuspid aortic valve patients to reduce paravalvular aortic regurgitation.

title: Transcatheter Aortic-Valve Replacement for Inoperable Severe Aortic Stenosis
key_finding: TAVR reduced death rates at 2 years compared to standard therapy (43.3% vs 68.0%) in inoperable patients with severe aortic stenosis, with sustained improvement in valve hemodynamics but higher stroke rates.
clinical_implications: TAVR provides durable survival benefit for appropriately selected inoperable patients with severe aortic stenosis, though extensive comorbidities may limit benefit.

title: Transcatheter versus Surgical Aortic-Valve Replacement in High-Risk Patients
key_finding: In high-risk patients with severe aortic stenosis, transcatheter and surgical aortic-valve replacement were associated with similar rates of survival at 1 year (24.2% vs 26.8% mortality), with transcatheter replacement being non-inferior to surgical replacement. Different periprocedural risk profiles were observed, with transcatheter replacement having more major vascular complications but less major bleeding and atrial fibrillation.
clinical_implications: Transcatheter aortic-valve replacement provides a viable alternative to surgical replacement in high-risk patients with severe aortic stenosis, offering similar survival outcomes with different risk-benefit profiles.

title: Transcatheter Aortic-Valve Implantation for Aortic Stenosis in Patients Who Cannot Undergo Surgery
key_finding: In patients with severe aortic stenosis unsuitable for surgery, TAVI significantly reduced 1-year mortality compared to standard therapy (30.7% vs 50.7%, HR 0.55, P<0.001). TAVI was associated with higher rates of major strokes and vascular complications but improved cardiac symptoms and reduced repeat hospitalizations.
clinical_implications: TAVI provides a viable treatment option for inoperable patients with severe aortic stenosis, offering significant survival benefit despite increased procedural risks.
